title: It’s starting…
summary: Matthew Berman posted a video titled "It's starting…" on YouTube, accompanied by promotional content for his newsletter and social channels. The exact topic of the commentary is not detailed in the provided text.
writeup: This video breaks down an Anthropic paper on recursive self-improvement, tracing how AI development has shifted from humans writing code directly to a chain of agents and sub-agents where the human is nearly abstracted out of the loop entirely. The through-line is a concrete progression: each layer of delegation produces exponentially more output, and the graphics in the paper make explicit that the only remaining bottleneck once the loop fully closes is compute. The video argues that engineering (writing and shipping code) is now the easy part, while the capacity for genuinely novel research ideas remains the missing ingredient separating today's agents from true recursive self-improvement.
key_points: Task horizon length for AI agents has been doubling roughly every four months, accelerating from an earlier doubling rate of every seven months: Claude Opus 3 (March 2024) completed tasks taking humans 4 minutes, Sonnet 3.7 completed 90-minute tasks, and Opus 4.6 completed 12-hour tasks, with the paper projecting day-length tasks in 2026 and week-length tasks in 2027.

On CoreBench, which tests a model's ability to reproduce results from novel research papers, AI systems went from a roughly 20% success rate in 2024 to near-100% saturation just 15 months later, demonstrating that reading and executing described research is now a solved capability.

Anthropic's own internal development illustrates the abstraction layers: early Claude was built by humans writing code directly; then chatbots mediated; then coding agents took over; then autonomous sub-agents parallelized the work; the fully closed loop, where Claude trains its own successor, removes the human entirely and leaves compute as the sole constraint.

The one missing ingredient that keeps recursive self-improvement from being realized today is the research side: deciding which experiments to run, interpreting results, and generating genuinely novel ideas, as opposed to executing well-specified tasks, which models now handle reliably.

The paper states that recursive self-improvement is not inevitable but also describes acceleration signals (AI-written code share rising, task horizons expanding, CoreBench saturating) that the video characterizes as the strongest hints of approaching recursive capability seen so far.
